segments.s083-g121: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این (literally 'on/over this') placed up front to modify the whole clause. بگذشت is the simple past, 3rd-person singular of گذشت used impersonally to mean 'passed' (of time). چندی is a duration adverb here ('for a while'), and سپهر ('the sky/heavens') is the noun tied to the verbal idea of time passing; the word order is a poetic/inverted sequence (prepositional phrase + verb + duration + noun) typical of classical Persian.
segments.s083-g122: This is a correlative negative construction using نه ... نه ('neither ... nor'). با + نوذر marks accompaniment/relation ('with Nowzar'), آرام is a predicate adjective ('calm/peaceful'), and بودش is بود + enclitic -ش ('it/he/that was'); the enclitic refers back to the situation or the preceding 'سپهر' (i.e. 'it was'). مهر here is a proper name/deity (not 'love'), so the clause reads 'it was not calm with Nowzar, nor with Mehr.' The second نه is omitted before مهر but implied by the correlative structure.
segments.s083-g25: همه (subject) = 'all/everything'. با توانایی او is a prepositional adjunct: با (with/by) + توانایی (ability) + او (his/he) — 'by his ability'. یکیست is the copular predicate (poetic fused form of 'یکی است'), meaning 'is one/the same'; Persian places the predicate at the end.
segments.s083-g26: اگر هست ... و گر ... is a conditional/alternate clause: اگر (if) + هست (there is/is) + بسیار (here a noun/adverb 'much/many') — 'if there is much'; و‌گر (poetic contraction of 'و اگر') introduces the alternative 'or if'; اندکیست is 'اندک است' fused = 'it is little'. The whole gives the alternative 'whether it is much or little.'
segments.s083-g27: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. از خداوند is a source phrase 'from God' (از + خداوند). خورشید و ماه is the coordinated noun phrase 'the sun and the moon' and functions as the agents/subjects of the following wish.
segments.s083-g28: ثنا (praise) is the noun being bestowed; بر is the preposition 'upon' and روان 'soul' is its object. منوچهر شاه is the name + title (Manuchehr, the king). The clause is an optative/wish: 'may praise be upon the soul of Manuchehr the king.' Note the lack of an explicit copula—classical Persian uses this order for blessings/wishes.
segments.s083-g29: ابر سام is a noun‑noun construction 'the cloud of Sam' (Sam's cloud). یل is an appositive 'hero' or vocative qualifier. باد here is not 'wind' but the optative particle 'باد' = 'may it be' (a common poetic wish). چندان modifies درود (so/greatly many blessings). The phrase expresses a wish: 'May Sam's cloud ... be such abundant blessings.'
segments.s083-g30: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that'. آید is the present 3rd‑person of آمدن 'to come'; همی is a classical adverb adding continuous/emphatic aspect ('indeed/continually' or here 'will/does come'); ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'; ابر باران فرود = 'a descent/fall of rain from the cloud' (باران + فرود, 'rain‑descent'). The clause means 'that from the cloud a rain‑fall comes down.'
segments.s083-g31: مران is a proper name. پهلوان is an appositive 'hero/champion' and جهاندیده is another epithet 'worldly‑wise' placed after it. را is the definite/object marker used here to mark the person who is the target of the following wish (common in poetic optatives). Together they name the person being prayed for.
segments.s083-g32: سرافراز is an adjective 'honored/exalted'. گرد is the short poetic form of the verb 'گردد' (subjunctive/optative 'become' / 'may it be') — here used elliptically to form a wish 'may ... be exalted'. پسندیده is 'the pleasing/approved/chosen one' and را again marks the target. The whole means 'may the chosen one be exalted.'
segments.s083-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. سوگ پدر is a genitive phrase 'the mourning of (his) father' with پدر ('father') in the normal noun position after the possessed noun سوگ. شاه نوذر is an appositive noun phrase identifying the father as 'King Nowzar'. بداشت is an archaic past verb here meaning 'held/observed' (classical past third-person), linking this whole clause as the time-setting for what follows.
segments.s083-g2: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (introducing source or origin). کیوان is used as a proper name here (a celestial/proper-name reference). کلاه کیی is a compound meaning 'Kayanian hat/crown' (کلاه 'hat/crown' modified by کیی 'Kayanian'). برفراشت is a past tense verb 'raised/erected' (بر‑فراشتن), so the phrase means 'he raised/placed the Kayanian crown from Kayvān.'
segments.s083-g3: به تخت منوچهر is a prepositional phrase 'onto/at the throne (for) Manuchehr' (Manuchehr is the person placed on the throne). بر بار داد is an idiomatic classical expression: داد (past 'gave/caused') with بر بار (literally 'upon the load') functions here as 'set (him) upon (the throne)' — a conventional way in older Persian to say 'installed on the throne.'
segments.s083-g4: بخواند here is the classical third-person past/preterite sense 'he summoned/called together' (from خواندن in the sense 'to call/summon'). انجمن را is the definite object 'the assembly' marked by را. و دینار داد coordinates the action: 'and he gave dinars' (داد = gave), i.e. distributed money to the assembly or attendants.
segments.s083-g5: برین برنیامد is a negative past construction: برنیامد (past 'did not come/occur/succeed'), with برین ('to this' / 'to that matter') pointing to the intended result — so the clause means 'this did not happen/was not realized.' بسی روزگار is an adverbial time phrase 'for many days / for a long time' (بسی = much/many, روزگار = days/time/fate).
segments.s083-g6: که introduces the consequence or continuing narrative ('so/that/when'). بیدادگر شد is 'became tyrannical' or 'became a tyrant' (بیدادگر = tyrant/cruel one; شد = became). سر شهریار uses سر in the sense 'head/chief/ruler' attached to شهریار ('the king' or ruler) — together indicating that the ruler assumed a tyrannical character.
segments.s083-g7: ز گیتی برآمد is a simple classical clause 'from the world/worldly realm there arose' (ز = from, گیتی = the world, برآمد = arose/issued forth). به هر جای غو is an adverbial phrase 'uproar/commotion in every place' (به هر جای = in every place, غو = tumult/up roar). Together: 'tumult arose everywhere in the world.'
segments.s083-g8: جهان را کهن شد is 'the world became old/aged' (را marks جهان as the affected object, کهن = old, شد = became). سر از شاه نو is a causal/agentive phrase 'on account of / because of the new king' where سر is used in the sense 'head/rule' or 'condition' linked with از + شاه نو; the whole clause reads idiomatically as 'the world was made aged because of the new king' (i.e. the new king's rule brought ruin/age to the world).
segments.s083-g41: نگهبان کشور به هنگام شاه is a noun phrase with modifiers: نگهبان (keeper/guardian) is the head noun, کشور (realm) follows as its complement ('keeper of the realm'). به هنگام is a fixed prepositional expression meaning 'at the time of' and governs شاه ('the king'); word order places the time-phrase after the possessive noun phrase in the poetic line.
segments.s083-g42: ازویست رخشنده فرخ کلاه is a verbless predicative clause common in Persian poetry: ازویست literally 'is from him/of him' (copular construction), followed by a sequence of adjectives رخشنده (radiant) and فرخ (auspicious/fortunate) that modify کلاه (hat/crown). The particle ازویست links the predicate to the possessor referenced earlier (i.e. 'is, for him...').
segments.s083-g43: کنون پادشاهی پرآشوب گشت is a complete clause: کنون 'now' sets time, پادشاهی 'the kingship/realm' is the subject, پرآشوب 'full of turmoil' is the predicate adjective, and گشت is the past/simple perfective of گشتن used copularly as 'became' (classical narrative prefers گشت for 'became').
segments.s083-g44: سخنها از اندازه اندر گذشت uses از + اندازه to mean 'beyond measure' (literally 'from measure'), اندر گذشت 'passed within' here idiomatically 'went beyond'. سخنها (plural) is the subject 'the speeches/words', and گذشت is the past verb 'passed/exceeded' placed at the end in typical Persian order.
segments.s083-g45: اگر برنگیرد وی آن گرز کین is a conditional clause: اگر 'if' introduces the condition. برنگیرد is the negative present/subjunctive (classical narrative uses it for a future/contingent sense) of برگشتن 'to turn back/withdraw'. وی is the third‑person pronoun ('he/it'), and آن گرز کین 'that mace (گرز) of kins/vengeance (کین)' functions as the object or topicalized instrument; poetic word order places the verb before the pronoun and the noun phrase.
segments.s083-g46: ازین تخت پردخته ماند زمین places the result: ازین (از + این) 'from this', تخت 'throne/platform', پردخته 'well‑wrought/forged' is an adjective qualifying the thing that falls, and ماند 'remained/was left' is the past verb with زمین 'ground' as its locative complement. The clause describes the mace ending up on the ground after leaving the throne.
segments.s083-g47: چو نامه‌بر سام نیرم رسید is a temporal clause: چو 'when' introduces the time; نامه‌بر سام is 'Sam's messenger' (possessive relation expressed without an explicit ezāfe in classical poetry), and نیرم is here a name/epithet attached to the messenger. رسید is the past tense 'arrived'. The sequence is compacted in poetic style (noun + possessor + epithet + verb).
segments.s083-g48: یکی باد سرد از جگر برکشید is a simple past-clause: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces باد 'wind' (the subject), سرد is the adjective 'cold', از جگر 'from the liver/heart' is a metaphorical source ('from the heart'), and برکشید is the past of برکشیدن 'to lift/raise' meaning here 'rose up' — idiomatically 'a chill rose in his heart.' Word order places the verb final as usual.
segments.s083-g33: همیشه (adverb) comes first and modifies the whole clause: “always.” دل و هوشش is a coordinated noun phrase; هوشش carries the enclitic -ش “his,” so the phrase reads “his heart and his intellect/wit.” آباد is a predicate adjective meaning “well/prosperous,” and باد here is the optative particle (classical/poetic) meaning “may (it be).” Word order is adverb → subject phrase → predicate → optative.
segments.s083-g34: روانش = روان + -ش, ‘his soul/spirit’; ز is the poetic preposition “from/of.” هر درد is a genitive/partitive phrase “every pain/affliction.” آزاد is the predicate adjective “free,” and باد again is the optative particle “may (it be).” The clause literally says “May his soul be free from every pain.”
segments.s083-g35: شناسد is a third‑person singular verbal form (classical) meaning “knows/would know.” مگر here functions as the restrictive particle “except/if not,” so the phrase reads “who would know except the champion of the world.” پهلوان جهان is a noun + epithet construction meaning “the world‑champion” (epithet follows noun).
segments.s083-g36: سخنها is the plural object “the words/matters.” هم adds emphasis/also “even/too.” از is the preposition “of/from” linking the following pair آشکار و نهان, which are coordinated opposites meaning “manifest (open) and hidden.” Together this supplies the object of the preceding verb: “the words, both manifest and hidden.”
segments.s083-g37: که تا functions as a temporal conjunction pair meaning “that/so that/until” (classical usage). شاه is the subject “the king.” مژگان به هم برنهاد is a poetic idiom: literally “put his eyelashes together,” i.e. blinked/closed his eyes; برنهاد is an archaic past form (بر‑ + نهاد) meaning “placed/raised/put” (here ‘brought together’).
segments.s083-g38: ز introduces the things remembered (“of/from Sam and Nariman”). سام نریمان are proper names in coordination. بسی here is adverbial classical usage meaning “much/often,” and کرد یاد is the compound verb “to make remembrance” = “remembered” (کرد is the light/past auxiliary and یاد the object ‘remembrance’).
segments.s083-g39: همیدون is the personal name and the clause begins with the copula (here written in contracted/poetic form as م representing هست). مرا (appearing here as the sequence م + را in the segmentation) indicates the benefactive/possessive ‘to me’/‘for me.’ پشت‌گرمی is the noun “backing/support,” and the whole clause reads “Hamīdūn is a support to me.” The copula is elided or contracted in classical style.
segments.s083-g40: که introduces an explanatory clause (“for/because”). هم پهلوانست is “he is also/indeed a champion” — پهلوانست is the fused form of پهلوان + است (archaic‑poetic -ست). و هم شاه دوست parallels that with “and (also) a friend of the king”; شاه modifies دوست to mean ‘the king’s friend’ or ‘the king’s favourite.’
segments.s083-g17: بترسید is a past/perfective 3rd-person singular form meaning “(he) was frightened” (verb before subject here, a common poetic inversion). بیدادگر is an epithet meaning “tyrant/cruel” modifying شهریار, which is the noun ‘king’ — together they form the subject phrase following the verb.
segments.s083-g18: فرستاد is a past 3rd-person singular verb “(he) sent/dispatched.” کس is an indefinite direct object “someone.” نزد is a preposition here meaning “to/in the presence of,” and سام is the proper name; سوار is an appositive epithet “rider/horseman” describing Sam.
segments.s083-g19: به marks location (here “in/at” the place), سگسار is used adjectivally/geographically “dog‑haunted” (a poetic epithet of the region), مازندران is the place name, بود is past copula “was,” and سام is the subject; word order (locative + بود + subject) is inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s083-g20: فرستاد again is past “(he) sent.” نوذر is the person sent (direct object). The first بر functions as a directional preposition “to/toward” (بر او = ‘to him’), او is the third‑person pronoun ‘him,’ and the second بر here marks the content or accompaniment (“bearing/with”) so بر پیام = ‘with a message’ (پیام = ‘message’).
segments.s083-g21: This nominative/vocative phrase lists divine or lofty names. خداوند is an honorific noun here ‘lord/sovereign,’ followed by the proper names کیوان, بهرام and هور joined by و (‘and’). The sequence addresses or invokes these figures.
segments.s083-g22: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” هست is the present/predicative copula “is.” آفریننده is the predicate noun “creator,” and پیل و مور are its direct objects in a poetic contrast ‘elephant and ant.’ The relative clause therefore attributes ‘creator of elephant and ant’ to the preceding lord.
segments.s083-g23: نه is the negative particle introducing the first half of a paired negation. دشواری is the noun “difficulty/hardship,” از is the preposition “from/of,” چیز is “thing/anything,” and برترمنش is an adjective or substantive meaning “the haughty/high‑minded”; together the phrase means roughly ‘no hardship (comes) from any thing for the proud,’ with the negation applied to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s083-g24: The second parallel clause begins with نه again. آسانی is “ease,” از is “from,” اندک is “small/little,” اندر is a locative/prepositional preform meaning “in/within,” and بوش here is used as a proper name/place or person reference; together the clause says that smallness does not provide ease ‘within Būsh’—a poetic balanced contrast to the first half.
segments.s083-g65: جهان (subject) + گردد (verb) + آباد (predicate adjective) is a simple predication: گرد(د) is the archaic/poetic 3rd‑person singular of 'گشت' meaning 'become' (here: 'will become' / 'is made'), and آباد follows it as the resultative adjective 'prosperous'. با داد او is a prepositional phrase 'with/by his justice' where داد (noun) takes the enclitic pronoun او 'his'. Word order (Subject–Verb–Predicate adjective then adjunct) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s083-g66: برویست is a poetic verb meaning 'rests upon / depends on' (verb placed before its nominal complement in poetic word order). ایران (the land/realm) is the noun governed by that verb; و بنیاد او coordinates a second noun phrase 'and its foundation' (بنیاد) with an enclitic او 'his/its'. Together: 'Iran and its foundation rest upon him.'
segments.s083-g67: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that' / 'so that'). ما is the subject; بنده باشیم is a nominal predicate with باشیم in the present/subjunctive ('may we be' / 'that we be') expressing state or wish. و فرمان کنیم is a coordinated clause — فرمان (noun 'command') precedes کنيم (1st‑person plural subjunctive/intentional 'we do/make/issue'), so together 'and we give commands'.
segments.s083-g68: روانها (plural 'souls/spirits') is the direct object; به مهرش is a prepositional phrase 'by/through his love' where مهر + enclitic ش = 'his love/affection'; گروگان is the noun 'hostage/pledge' and کنيم is the verb 'we make/take' (1st‑person plural), so the clause literally says 'we make souls hostages by his love.' Word order (object → adjunct → object noun → verb) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s083-g69: بدیشان ('to them') is a prepositional/dative form; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' that modifies the following verb; گفت is the verb 'said' (here used in narrative past), and سام سوار follows as the subject in apposition ('Sam, the horseman'). The verb–subject order (گفت سام سوار) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s083-g70: که here introduces the content of what was said (an indirect question). این ('this') is fronted for emphasis, کی ('who') is the interrogative subject, and پسندد is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of 'پسندیدن' meaning 'would approve/accept'. ز من is the prepositional phrase 'of/from me' and کردگار ('Creator') stands at the end as the addressee (a vocative). The clause therefore reads: 'Who, O Creator, would approve this from me?'
segments.s083-g71: که چون is a subordinating phrase 'that when / since when'. نوذری is a nominative noun meaning 'a votive/one given by vow' and is specified by the following prepositional phrase از نژاد کیان ('from the lineage/race of the Kians'). The whole chunk means 'when a votive (one) of the Kayan line ...'.
segments.s083-g72: به تخت کیی is 'onto the Kayan throne' (به + noun phrase). The sequence بر کمر بر میان is a pair of locatives introduced by repeated بر: بر کمر 'upon the waist' and بر میان 'in the middle/center' — a compressed poetic description of how the person is seated or girded (the verb is omitted by ellipsis and understood from the previous clause). The repeated preposition and omission of the verb are typical of Ferdowsi's elevated/condensed diction.
segments.s083-g81: This phrase reads as دلش + گر (an archaic/poetic clitic): دلش 'his heart' and گر 'if'. راه پدر is a genitive phrase 'father's way/path', and گشت باز is a verbal sequence where گشت (past of گشتن) = 'turned/became' and باز = 'back/again'. Word order reflects older/poetic Persian: conditional particle 'گر' attaches to دلش, then the thing turned from (ز راه پدر) and the verb (گشت) comes before the adverb (باز).
segments.s083-g82: برین‌بر functions adverbially ('to/onto that place' or 'to that matter') and governs the following verb نیامد 'did not come'. زمانی دراز is a temporal adverbial 'for a long time' (زمانی = 'for a while/time', دراز = 'long'), placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s083-g83: هنوز 'still/yet' is the sentential adverb. آهنی is a predicate noun/adjective 'iron (made of iron)'. نیست negates the predicate. زنگار خورد is a compound verbal idea 'rust-eaten/has taken rust' (زنگار + خورد), with خورد functioning as the verb forming the idiom 'زنگار خوردن' = 'to get rust'.
segments.s083-g84: که introduces a subordinate/consequential clause ('which/so that/so that it may be the case'). رخشنده is an adjectival/participial description 'shining, radiant'. دشوار is a predicate adjective 'difficult'. شایدش is composed of modal شاید 'perhaps/it may' with enclitic -ش 'it/him', and کرد is the light causative/lexical verb 'to make/cause' — together 'شایدش کرد' = 'may make it'.
segments.s083-g85: من 'I' is the explicit subject. آن ایزدی فره is the object phrase 'that divine farr (divine glory)'. باز here combines with آورم to mean 'restore' or 'bring back'. آورم is first‑person present/subjunctive/optative form functioning here as a declarative future/intentional 'I will bring/restore'. Word order places the object before the verb in the expected Persian SOV pattern (with poetic flexibility).
segments.s083-g86: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' with the object marker را. به مهرش is a prepositional phrase 'by/through his love/favor' (مهرش = his love/grace). نیاز آورم is a light causative/result clause where نیاز 'need/longing' is the state induced and آورم 'I will bring' means 'I will cause' — together 'I will make the world feel need/yearn (for him) by his love.'
segments.s083-g87: شما is the addressee 'you' (plural/formal). بر گذشته = 'about/on the past' (بر here = about/upon). پشیمان شوید uses the verb شدن in the subjunctive/jussive form شوید 'become/let it be that you are' — here a hortative/imperative sense: 'become remorseful' or 'be sorry'.
segments.s083-g88: به نوی = 'into/for new' (به + نوی) signals taking up something anew. ز سر here = 'from the head/from the outset' (idiomatically 'from the start'), باز = 'again/afresh', and پیمان شوید again uses شوید (jussive/subjunctive of شدن) with the sense 'enter into / become pledged' — overall: 'enter into a new covenant again from the beginning.'
segments.s083-g57: ز is the poetic causal preposition 'because of'. بیدادی is a noun meaning 'injustice/tyranny' and is the object of ز. نوذر is the personal name Nowzar; تاجور is an epithet attached to the name (here treated as a noun in apposition). Together the phrase means 'because of the injustice of Nowzar Tajur.'
segments.s083-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which' referring back to نوذر. بر + خیره here forms an adverbial phrase meaning 'in a dazed/astounded state' (بر used idiomatically 'on/in'). گم کرد is a light‑verb construction: گم 'lost' + کرد 'to cause/do' → 'caused to be lost' (i.e. 'lost/ruined'). راه پدر is the object 'the father's way/ancestral path.'
segments.s083-g59: جهان (subject) + گشت (verb 'became') + ویران 'ruined' gives 'the world became ruined.' ز کردار اوی is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of his deeds' (ز = 'because of', اوی = possessive 'his').
segments.s083-g60: غنوده شد is a light‑verb construction meaning 'fell into slumber/was made to sleep' (غنوده 'asleep' + شد 'became'). آن بخت بیدار اوی is a noun phrase 'that fortune, (which had been) awake, of his' — بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert' modifying بخت, and اوی is the possessive 'his.'
segments.s083-g61: بگردد همی is a present/habitual literary form: ب‑ + گَرد + همی gives 'keeps turning/keeps going.' From there از ره بخردی is an adverbial phrase 'from the way/path of wisdom' (از = 'from', ره = 'way', بخردی = 'wisdom'). The whole clause means 'keeps turning away from the path of wisdom.'
segments.s083-g62: ازو ('from him' / 'of him') + دور شد ('became distant') + فره ایزدی ('divine glory'). Word order places the prepositional source first; the predicate 'دور شد' takes فره ایزدی as its subject: 'the divine glory became distant from him.'
segments.s083-g63: چه باشد اگر is a fixed hypothetical formula 'what would it be if...' with باشد in the subjunctive/hypothetical sense. سام is the name; یل پهلوان are two epithets in apposition (both mean 'hero/champion'), intensifying his heroic status.
segments.s083-g64: نشیند is the present/subjunctive 3rd‑person form 'were to sit' / 'should sit.' برین is orthographic contraction of بر این ('upon this'); تخت is 'throne'; روشن‌روان is a compound epithet ('bright‑souled' / 'radiant'), modifying تخت. Together: 'were to sit upon this radiant/illustrious throne.'
segments.s083-g89: گر is the classical conditional particle 'if'. آمرزش is the noun 'forgiveness' which here is the thing being (not) obtained; کردگار سپهر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the Creator of the heavens' (کردگار = Creator/God, سپهر = heavens) placed after the noun it modifies in a poetic order.
segments.s083-g90: نیابید is the negative form of یابیدن used here with a subjunctive/optative flavour — 'does not find/obtain' (classical narrative conditional). و links the next clause. از introduces the source 'from'. نوذر is the proper name Nowzar, followed by شاه as his title; مهر here means 'favor/kindness' that one might receive from the king.
segments.s083-g91: بدین گیتی اندر is a poetic way to say 'in this world' (بدین = 'in this', گیتی = 'world', اندر = 'in/within'). بود is the past copula 'was'. خشم شاه is the possessive phrase 'the king's anger' (noun + possessor after it in classical Persian).
segments.s083-g92: به + infinitive برگشتن ('to return') forms a purposive/nominal construction 'for the returning of fire' or 'in the return of fire'; آتش is the noun 'fire'. بود جایگاه literally 'there was a place/seat' — i.e. the place/status or consequence was the return of fire (جایگاه = 'place').
segments.s083-g93: بزرگان 'the great ones' (nobles/elders) is the subject. ز + کرده marks the reason/thing causing remorse: 'because of (that which was) done' (ز = 'from/because of', کرده = 'the deed/what was done'). پشیمان شدند is the past-tense verbal phrase 'became/are regretful' (شدند = past plural of شدن).
segments.s083-g94: یکایک means 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. ز marks separation/'from' and together with باز (here 'free') and سر forms an idiom meaning they freed themselves from the obligation — 'withdrew from the pledge'. پیمان is 'covenant/oath' and شدند is the past plural 'they became/they did so'. The phrase thus means 'one by one they renounced/broke the covenant.'
segments.s083-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is past 'came'. به درگاه سام is the prepositional phrase 'to Sam's court' (درگاه = royal court/presence, سام = name). سوار (placed after the court for poetic word order) is 'the rider/horseman' — the subject of آمد.
segments.s083-g96: پذیره here denotes reception/being received; شدش is an archaic verbal form where -ش is the enclitic object 'him' and شد (past of شدن) gives the resultive sense 'was received' or literally 'it befell him (that he was received)'. نوذر شهریار is the apposition 'Nowzar the king' who performs the reception; in Persian the agent/title can follow the verbal phrase in narrative ordering.
segments.s083-g105: به نوذر is a prepositional indirect-object phrase ('to/for Nowzar') with به + name. در پندها uses در in the sense 'door/gate' + plural پندها 'admonitions', and را is the definite/direct-object marker attaching to that noun phrase. گشاد is a past/resultative adjective/verb 'opened' placed after the object in the classical/poetic SOV order.
segments.s083-g106: سخنهای نیکو is the noun phrase 'good sayings/words' (سخن + plural -ها, adjective نیکو). بسی is an adverbial intensifier 'much/many' modifying the verb phrase. کرد is the light verb 'did/made' that combines with the noun یاد to form یاد کردن 'to remember/recall'; Persian often uses noun + کردن as the predicate, with کرد carrying tense.
segments.s083-g107: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گرد here names the 'circle/retinue' or 'around' (i.e., company), followed by the names فریدون و هوشنگ and the title شاه; word order (preposition + noun phrase) is characteristic of the style and places the possessor names immediately after گرد.
segments.s083-g108: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' linking this clause to the previous material. از منوچهر is another 'from Manuchehr' phrase, and زیبای گاه is a poetic epithet 'beautiful/fair of the time' with adjective preceding the noun for emphasis.
segments.s083-g109: که begins a relative clause 'who/that'. گیتی is the noun 'the world' as part of the clause's content. بداد و دهش are two coordinated nouns (here 'injustice/oppression' and 'gift/grant' per the listed senses), and داشتند is the past-tense plural 'they had/possessed' placed at the clause end (classical SOV).
segments.s083-g110: به here functions adverbially ('by/with') introducing the manner (by injustice). بیداد 'injustice' is the noun governed by به. بر چشم is a spatial expression 'upon the eye' (بر + noun). نگماشتند is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning 'they placed/set/turned (their gaze)'; the verb appears at the end as the finite predicate.
segments.s083-g111: دل او is the possessor + noun 'his heart' (subject/affected). ز کژی is a prepositional phrase 'from crookedness/wrong' (ز = from/because of). به داد is the target phrase 'to justice' (به + noun). آورید is a past-form verb 'brought/turned' (they brought/caused), completing the action; together the phrase means 'they restored his heart from wrong to right.'
segments.s083-g112: چنان کرد is 'thus did' (چنان = so/thus; کرد = did), with the subject نوذر following the verb by poetic inversion. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'; او رای دید is an idiomatic sequence 'he saw/decided (his) counsel/opinion' — رای + دید functions as 'he deemed/decided it so.'
segments.s083-g49: This is a temporal prepositional phrase. به ... هنگام = 'at the time of ...'; شبگیر functions adverbially ('night‑fall/nocturnal') and هنگام is the explicit temporal noun 'time (of)'. بانگِ خروس is a genitive (sound of the rooster) following هنگام; the whole phrase means 'at the time of the rooster's cry.'
segments.s083-g50: برآمد is a past tense verb meaning 'arose/began/issued forth.' It is followed by the infinitival/verbal noun خروشیدن ('to roar/sound'), so the combination denotes that a loud sounding began. بوق و کوس are the instruments (trumpet and drum); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'.
segments.s083-g51: یکی before a noun is the indefinite 'a certain' (classical usage). لشکری is a collective noun 'army/host.' راند is the simple past of راندن ('drove/marched/advanced') and از + گرگسار gives the origin/place 'from the wolf‑haunted area.' Word order is subject phrase (one army) + verb + origin phrase.
segments.s083-g52: که introduces a relative/consequence clause linked to the preceding clause ('so that/which'). دریای سبز is a poetic compound ('green sea' = verdant plain). اندرو is an archaic locative 'in it/there' and گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became' or 'was turned into.' خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised/laid low' — together 'the green plain there became humbled/desolated.'
segments.s083-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' نزدیک ایران is an adverbial phrase 'near Iran' (adjective before noun as is common), رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived,' and آن سپاه is the demonstrative + noun 'that army/host' (the subject), placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis.
segments.s083-g54: پذیره is a noun/participial stem 'reception/welcoming.' شد(ند) is the past plural 'they became' used here as an auxiliary to form the verbal idea; the enclitic -ش marks 'him' (object). Grammatically this yields 'they received him.' بزرگان is the subject 'the nobles/great ones' and به راه is the locative 'on the road' (where the reception happened).
segments.s083-g55: پیاده here is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'on foot.' همه is the subject 'all/everyone.' پیش سام دلیر is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the presence of / before Sam the brave' — پیش = 'before/in the presence of,' and دلیر is a postpositive epithet 'brave' modifying Sam.
segments.s083-g56: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/advanced.' و is the coordinator 'and.' گفتند is 'they said' (reported speech follows). هر گونه is an idiomatic phrase 'in whatever manner/however,' and دیر here means 'late' — together in direct speech it functions roughly as 'however late it may be' (an apologetic or concessive formula).
segments.s083-g97: به marks a dative/target ('for/to') and precedes the name; فرخ here is used as a proper name/epithet. پی is a short poetic/linking particle meaning 'after' or 'following' and here links the name to its appositive title, so the phrase reads as 'for Farrokh, the renowned champion.' نامور and پهلوان are appositive adjectives/nouns ('renowned' + 'champion/hero') attached after the name.
segments.s083-g98: جهان is the subject 'the world.' سر به سر is a reduplicated expression intensifying completeness; سر به سر شد is a past passive/ intransitive use of شد 'became' meaning 'was (made) entirely/wholly so.' به before نوی functions as 'into' (change into), نوی جوان = 'new, youthful' (نوی 'new' modifying جوان 'young/youthful'). The clause therefore says the world 'became wholly into new youth' (i.e. was renewed, became youthful).
segments.s083-g99: به پوزش is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with obeisance/respect'; مهان is the subject 'the guests/visitors.' پیش نوذر = 'before Nowzar / in Nowzar's presence.' شدند is the past plural of شدن used here to report their coming/presentation (literary use: 'they presented themselves' / 'they became before Nowzar'). Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + subject + prepositional phrase + finite verb.
segments.s083-g100: به جان و به دل is a paired adverbial phrase 'in (their) soul and in (their) heart' (emphatic dative). ویژه here functions adverbially 'especially/particularly.' کهتر شد(ند) is past plural of شدن with کهتر 'smaller/lesser' used metaphorically: 'they grew smaller' = 'they humbled themselves.' The clause thus says they became especially humble in heart and soul.
segments.s083-g101: برافروخت is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'kindled/was inflamed' (here 'became angry' / 'flushed with anger'); the verb precedes the subject نوذر (inverted VSO common in poetry). ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' تخت 'throne' is the object of ز, and مهی is an adjective 'moon‑like / beautiful' modifying تخت. The phrase therefore: 'Nowzar was inflamed and (rose) from the moonlike throne.'
segments.s083-g102: نشست is the past of نشستن 'sat down / took a seat.' اندر آرام is an adverbial phrase 'in calm / quietly.' با + فرهی means 'with the noble(s)' (با = 'with'; فرهی here denotes the noble/cultured person(s)). The clause states the resulting action: he sat down calmly among (or with) the nobles.
segments.s083-g103: جهان‌پهلوان is a compound subject 'world‑champion / mighty hero.' پیش نوذر = 'in the presence of Nowzar / before Nowzar.' به پای is an idiomatic locative 'at the feet of' indicating reverence (به = 'to/at', پای = 'foot'). Word order places the honorific subject first, then the locative phrase.
segments.s083-g104: پرستنده او بود is a clause with predicate noun 'پرستنده' (worshipper/devoted follower) + pronoun او 'him' and past copula بود 'was' — 'he was his worshipper.' و هم رهنمای adds 'and also (هم) his guide/adviser' (رهنمای). The structure lists two roles the hero fulfills toward Nowzar.
segments.s083-g113: دل مهتران را بدو نرم کرد = literal order: 'heart of the chiefs (را) to-him softened made.' را is the direct-object marker attaching دل مهتران (the hearts of the chiefs). بدو is a fused prepositional pronoun (به + او) meaning 'to him/toward him.' نرم کرد is past tense: نرم (adjective 'soft/gentle') plus کرد ('made/did'), a common Persian causative pattern meaning 'caused to become soft' → 'softened.'
segments.s083-g114: همه داد و بنیاد آزرم کرد = here همه is the object 'everything/all.' داد is the past verb 'gave' (not the noun 'justice') and is coordinated by و with the next verbal clause. بنیاد آزرم is a noun phrase 'the Azram foundation' (آزرم treated as a name), and کرد is the second past verb 'established/made' — so: 'he gave all and founded the Azram foundation.'
segments.s083-g115: چو گفته شد از گفتنیها همه = a temporal subordinate clause: چو 'when' + گفته شد (passive/perfect 'was said' or 'had been told'). از گفتنیها همه is a genitive/partitive phrase 'all of the things to be said' (گفتنیها = 'the sayable / matters to be said'), placed after the verb for poetic word-order.
segments.s083-g116: به گردنکشان و به شاه رمه = two prepositional recipients introduced by به 'to/for': گردنکشان (plural) here means 'the rebels' or 'the proud/insubordinate ones,' and به شاه رمه 'to the king's throng/host' (رمه = throng/herd). The phrase names the parties to whom the matters were communicated or concerned.
segments.s083-g117: برون‌رفت با خلعت نوذری = برون‌رفت is used as 'the outcome/result' (solution/outcome). با 'with' introduces the accompanying item خلعت 'a robe of honour' and نوذری qualifies it as 'votive' or 'pertaining to a vow' (i.e. a votive robe). The phrase sets the means/accompaniment for what follows.
segments.s083-g118: چه تخت و چه تاج و چه انگشتری = a classical enumerative construction: repeated چه here functions like 'whether/whatever' or simply to list items emphatically. تخت 'throne', تاج 'crown', انگشتری 'ring' are the regalia being enumerated; the construction links these items as the components of the 'outcome' introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s083-g119: غلامان و اسپان زرین ستام = غلامان 'the servants/pages' and اسپان 'the horses' form the coordinated subject. زرین 'golden' modifies (either غلامان or اسپان or both) and ستام is effectively a past/passive participial notion 'praised' or 'adorned' here; the clause describes how the pages and steeds were splendid (golden and praised/adorned).
segments.s083-g120: پر از گوهر سرخ زرین دو جام = پر از 'full of' + گوهر 'gems' (سرخ 'red' qualifies the gems). زرین modifies دو جام 'two golden cups.' Word order is poetic: the noun phrase 'دو جام' (two cups) is described as golden (زرین) and full of red gems, i.e. 'two golden cups full of red jewels.'
segments.s083-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; او is the third-person pronoun 'he'; رسمهای پدر is a genitive noun phrase 'the rites/customs of (his) father' with the plural -ها (here written as -های) attached to رسم; درنوشت is a past/preterite verb meaning 'wrote down/inscribed' and completes the clause, so word order is Conjunction + Subject + Object(genitive) + Verb (typical older/poetic Persian sequencing).
segments.s083-g10: This clause is verb‑initial (poetic inversion): ابا here is used as an intransitive verb 'to refuse / be reluctant' (they objected); موبدان و ردان is the compound plural subject 'the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests) and the rādān/opponents'; تیز is an adjectival predicate 'sharp/keen/intense' and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became,' so the clause means 'the priests and opponents became vehement in refusal.'
segments.s083-g11: همی is a continuous/iterative particle here meaning 'ever' or marking ongoing state; مردمی functions as 'the people' (collective noun); نزد او is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence / before him'; خوار شد is a predicative construction with خوار 'despised/humbled' and شد 'became' (past), so literally 'the people became despised before him.'
segments.s083-g12: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش); برده is used adjectivally/participial here as 'carried away / seized' (i.e., his heart was taken); گنج و دینار names the agents/objects 'treasure and dinars (coins)'; شد functions as the past of گشتن/شدن giving the resultive sense — together meaning 'his heart was carried away by treasure and coins' (i.e. he was carried away by greed).
segments.s083-g13: کدیور is a proper name (subject/topic); یکایک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one / each in turn'; سپاهی شدند is a compound predicate with سپاهی 'soldiers/warriors' as predicative noun and شدند (past plural of شدن) 'became,' so the clause reads 'Kadiur's (men) one by one became soldiers.'
segments.s083-g14: دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave ones'; سزاوار is a predicative adjective 'worthy / deserving of'; شاهی here denotes 'kingship' (a noun) and شدند is the past plural 'they became,' so the clause means 'the brave became worthy of kingship.' Note the adjective‑noun‑verb order typical of Persian predicative constructions.
segments.s083-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از روی کشور is a prepositional phrase 'from the face/surface of the country' (از + روی + کشور); برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth'; خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' and functions as the subject/object of برآمد depending on emphasis — overall 'when a cry rose from the land.'
segments.s083-g16: جهانی سراسر forms an emphatic phrase 'the whole world throughout' (جهانی 'worldly/universal' + سراسر 'throughout'); برآمد is again 'arose/issued forth'; به جوش is a prepositional/complimentary phrase meaning 'into a boil' or 'into tumult/ferment' (به + جوش), so the clause says the whole world 'rose into a seething turmoil.'
segments.s083-g73: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'for kingship/for becoming king'. The sequence مرا را تاج should be read idiomatically as 'a crown for me' (object/topic marking with را following the pronominal object), followed by the modal باید 'is necessary/must'. بسود (literally 'to/for benefit') qualifies what kind of crown is needed. The clause order is slightly archaic: purpose/topic first, then the thing needed, then the necessity and its desirable property.
segments.s083-g74: محالست is an older or poetic form meaning 'it is impossible' (محال + ـست 'is'). و links a contrasting clause. این کس 'this person/man' is the topical subject. نیارد شنود is inverted classical phrasing: شنود ('hears' / 'will listen') is the verb and نیارد here functions as a negative/oblique verb form (an older orthography of نیاورد/نشنود sense) meaning 'will not take/accept/bring' — together the clause conveys 'he will not hear/accept it.'
segments.s083-g75: خود این گفت is emphatic: خود 'himself/very' + این 'this' + گفت (classical verb 'said') = 'he himself said this.' یارد کس اندر جهان is an exclamatory apposition meaning 'the beloved/companion of someone in the world' — here یارد = 'beloved/companion' and کس = 'anyone/person'.
segments.s083-g76: چنین introduces the following rhetorical question 'such/like this'. زهره here uses the classical image of Venus (planet/goddess) to mean special charm or fortunate aspect. دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/possesses'. کس اندر نهان asks 'does anyone in secret/hiddenly' — اندر نهان = 'in hiding / secretly'. The whole line questions whether anyone secretly has such a fortunate charm.
segments.s083-g77: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. دختری از منوچهر شاه = 'a daughter of Manuchehr the king' with the possessive/relational از marking origin/lineage. The indefinite -ی on دختر indicates 'a daughter/one daughter' rather than 'the daughter.'
segments.s083-g78: بران is a locative/adverbial 'upon that / on it' (pointing back to the throne). تخت زرین 'golden throne' is a nominal phrase (treating زرین as adjectival 'golden'). شدی (a past/optative form of شدن) here carries the conditional/hypothetical sense 'should sit/become' and با کلاه 'with a crown' is an instrumental accompaniment phrase; the clause describes the hypothetical situation of her sitting on the golden throne wearing a crown.
segments.s083-g79: نبودی جز از خاک بالین من is a conditional/result clause: نبودی (conditional/past form 'you would be / you would not be' depending on context) + جز 'except' yields 'you would be nothing except...' از خاک بالین من 'from the dust beside my pillow' is an idiom meaning a lowly or negligible position (literally 'the dust of my pillow'). Word order places the exception clause after the verb for emphasis, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s083-g80: بدو 'to him/at that' (classical dative) + شاد بودی 'you would have been glad' — the modal/temporal sense is conditional/past-subjunctive. جهانبین من is a vocative or apposition 'my world-seer' (an epithet for the addressee). Put together: 'you would have rejoiced at that, my world-seeing one.'

sentences.s083-s61: A while passed over this as well; it was at peace with neither Nowzar nor Mehr.
sentences.s083-s13: All is the same by his ability, whether it be great or small.
sentences.s083-s14: Now, from God, may the sun and the moon's praise be upon the soul of King Manuchehr.
sentences.s083-s15: May Sam's cloud, O hero, bring so many blessings that from the cloud a rain‑fall comes down.
sentences.s083-s16: May Maran, the worldly‑wise hero, be exalted; may the chosen one be raised high.
sentences.s083-s1: When King Nowzar held the mourning for his father, he raised the Kayanian crown from Kayvān.
sentences.s083-s2: He placed Manuchehr on the throne, summoned the assembly and gave out dinars.
sentences.s083-s3: For many days this did not come to pass; then the ruler became a tyrant:
sentences.s083-s4: From the world there rose tumult in every place; the world became aged because of the new king.
sentences.s083-s21: The keeper of the realm, at the king's hour, is his shining, auspicious crown.
sentences.s083-s22: Now the kingship became tumultuous; words passed beyond all measure.
sentences.s083-s23: If that mace of vengeance does not turn back, it will be hurled from this well‑wrought throne and lie on the ground.
sentences.s083-s24: When Sam's messenger Nīram arrived, a cold wind rose from his heart.
sentences.s083-s17: May his heart and his wit always be well; may his soul be free from every pain.
sentences.s083-s18: Who would know (it), if not the champion of the world, the words both manifest and hidden?
sentences.s083-s19: Until the king drew his eyelashes together (i.e. closed his eyes), he recalled Sam and Nariman often.
sentences.s083-s20: Hamīdūn is a support to me, for he is both a champion and a friend of the king.
sentences.s083-s9: The tyrant king was frightened; he sent someone to Sam the horseman.
sentences.s083-s10: Sam was in the dog‑haunted Mazandaran; the king sent Nowzar to him with a message.
sentences.s083-s11: "O lord Kayvān and Bahrām and Hūr, who is the creator of elephant and ant,"
sentences.s083-s12: Neither hardship comes from any thing for the haughty, nor does any smallness bring ease within Būsh.
sentences.s083-s33: With his justice the world will become prosperous; Iran and its foundations rest upon him.
sentences.s083-s34: that we be servants and give commands; we will make souls hostages to his love.
sentences.s083-s35: Sam the horseman thus said to them: 'Who, O Creator, would approve this of me?'
sentences.s083-s36: that when, as a votive from the lineage of the Kians, (one) is upon the Kayan throne — a girdle about the waist, in the middle.
sentences.s083-s41: If his heart turned away from his father's path, he did not return there for a long time.
sentences.s083-s42: The iron has not yet been touched by rust; that may make it hard to make it shine.
sentences.s083-s43: I will restore that divine farr (glory); I will make the world yearn for him through his love.
sentences.s083-s44: Be remorseful for the past; renew your covenant afresh from the start.
sentences.s083-s29: Because of the injustice of Nowzar Tajur, who, in a daze, lost his father's way.
sentences.s083-s30: The world became ruined by his deeds; his once‑awake fortune fell asleep.
sentences.s083-s31: He keeps turning away from the path of wisdom; the divine glory became distant from him.
sentences.s083-s32: What would it be if Sam, the hero and champion, were to sit upon this bright‑souled throne?
sentences.s083-s45: If the forgiveness of the Creator of the heavens is not obtained, and one does not receive favor from Nowzar the king,
sentences.s083-s46: In this world the king's wrath existed; its place (or consequence) was the returning/rekindling of fire.
sentences.s083-s47: The great ones were remorseful for what they had done; one by one they withdrew from (or broke) the covenant.
sentences.s083-s48: When a horseman came to Sam's court, Nowzar the king received him.
sentences.s083-s53: He opened for Nowzar the doors of counsel and recalled many good sayings.
sentences.s083-s54: From the circle of Fereydun and Hushang the king, and likewise from Manuchehr — fair in his day.
sentences.s083-s55: who, though they possessed the world and its bounty, cast an unjust gaze upon the eye.
sentences.s083-s56: They turned his heart from crookedness to justice; thus Nowzar acted as he judged.
sentences.s083-s25: At nightfall, at the cock's crow, horns and war‑drums began to sound.
sentences.s083-s26: A certain host marched out from the wolf‑haunted region, so that the green plain there was laid low.
sentences.s083-s27: When that host drew near Iran, the nobles received him on the road.
sentences.s083-s28: All on foot went before the brave Sam and said, 'however late it may be.'
sentences.s083-s49: For Farrokh, the famed champion, the whole world became entirely renewed and youthful.
sentences.s083-s50: The guests, with obeisance, came before Nowzar; in heart and soul they were made especially humble.
sentences.s083-s51: Nowzar flamed up (in anger) and rose from his moonlike throne; he sat down in calm among the nobles.
sentences.s083-s52: The world‑champion stood before Nowzar at his feet, a devoted worshipper of him and likewise his counsellor.
sentences.s083-s57: He softened the hearts of the chiefs toward him; he gave everything and founded the Azram foundation.
sentences.s083-s58: When all the things to be said had been spoken, (they were told) to the rebels and to the king's throng.
sentences.s083-s59: The outcome was, with the votive robe, the throne, the crown, and the ring.
sentences.s083-s60: The servants and the horses were golden and praised; two golden cups, full of red gems.
sentences.s083-s5: When he wrote down his father's rites, the mobeds and the rādān grew sharp in their refusal.
sentences.s083-s6: The people were humbled before him; his heart was carried away by treasure and dinars.
sentences.s083-s7: Kadiur's men, one by one, became warriors; the brave became worthy of kingship.
sentences.s083-s8: When from the face of the country a cry arose, the whole world throughout burst into a seething tumult.
sentences.s083-s37: For kingship I need a crown that will profit me — it is impossible, and this man will not hear of it.
sentences.s083-s38: He himself said this: 'Is he anyone's beloved in the world!' — Does anyone secretly possess such a Venus?
sentences.s083-s39: If a daughter of King Manuchehr should sit upon that golden throne with a crown,
sentences.s083-s40: you would be nothing but the dust beside my pillow; you would have rejoiced for him, my world-seer.
